faqs.a: A systematic review is an attempt to answer a single, focused question by finding and evaluating every relevant study, not just the ones that are easy to find or happen to agree. It's a structured, repeatable process for pulling the whole body of evidence together.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a systematic review and a meta-analysis?
faqs.a: A systematic review is the structured process of finding and appraising all the relevant studies. A meta-analysis is the statistical method for combining their results into a single estimate, and it's often part of a systematic review though the two aren't the same thing.

faqs.q: Does a systematic review guarantee the truth?
faqs.a: No. A systematic review is only as good as the studies it includes and the care of the people who did it. A careful review of weak studies still rests on weak ground, though it remains one of the strongest forms of evidence available.
